title: _hooklib.py accretion: 773 LOC spanning six unrelated concern clusters in the most-consumed module
evidence: _hooklib.py is imported by essentially every entry script (>10 consumers — the ai-markers critical-shared-dependency threshold) and now bundles: security regexes (CRYPTO_RE/SECRET_RE, 93-123), protected-path classification (126-259), stdio/JSON plumbing (262-338), host delegation (76-84, 341-353), a full glob-scope engine with two layers of caching (401-623), atomic writes (626-649), gate-event logging + block/remind/warn (660-712), and flow-staleness heuristics (715-773). Each addition is individually documented, but the module has become the default landing zone — e.g. the glob engine (~220 LOC with its perf caches) is a self-contained subsystem with exactly three consumers (is_migration_path/is_ci_path/is_deploy_path).
impact: Every change to any cluster rides the highest-blast-radius file in the codebase (synced into two plugin payloads, imported by ~20 entries), maximizing the review/regression surface per edit and inviting further accretion. The repo's own convention (coding-standards.md: shared logic in purpose-named _<name>lib.py modules) supports splitting.
recommendation: Carve the glob-scope engine into a _scopelib.py (its consumers are three thin wrappers) and consider _gateeventlib for the logging/block/remind/warn sink; keep _hooklib as the thin common surface. No behavior change — a sync-core run plus import updates.
acceptance_criteria: _hooklib.py drops below ~500 LOC with the glob engine extracted
No entry-script behavior change (existing suites green)
